verdict_reasoning: LYN is a Src-family tyrosine kinase canonically localized to the cytoplasm via N-terminal myristoylation and palmitoylation, anchoring it to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane and intracellular membranes. However, LYN has documented surface display in at least two critical contexts: (1) activated B cells and immune cells undergoing B-cell-receptor (BCR) signaling, where myristoylated / palmitoylated LYN transiently relocates to the outer leaflet or remains clustered at TCR/BCR signaling microclusters on the PM; (2) cancer cells (particularly B-cell lymphomas and chronic myeloid leukemia, where LYN is often dysregulated or overexpressed), where LYN exhibits ecto-kinase activity and surface display. Additionally, LYN can be externalized during apoptosis and necroptosis. These non-baseline surface pools qualify as cell_state_induced. The baseline compartment is inner-leaflet / cytoplasmic, but the documented activation-induced and cancer-state translocation to the surface meets the threshold for contextual.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Unclear whether the 'surface' pool during BCR signaling reflects stable outer-leaflet translocation vs. transient clustering at inner-leaflet signaling assemblies; cancer-cell ecto-LYN surface activity is well-documented but frequency / prevalence in primary tumors vs. cell-line artifacts remains uncertain.
